content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 24, 2008

Michael J. O'Neill
Trustee, Pacifica School District
1562 Miranda Ct.
Pacifica, CA 94044

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-097

Dear Mr. O'Neill:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is confined to the
Act and we, therefore, offer no advice on other laws that could be applicable, such as
Elections Code Section 18680.

QUESTIONS

1. May a committee that was primarily formed to support a school parcel tax
measure on the June 3, 2008, ballot retain funds it has after the election to support
an anticipated future school parcel tax measure for the same school district?

2. If the committee is not allowed to retain its surplus funds to support a measure in
the future, may the funds be donated to the school district or to the Education
Foundation, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization established under federal law?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes, a ballot measure committee may retain funds left over from the election to
support an anticipated ballot measure for the same purpose in a future election.

2. Since question one is answered in the positive, an answer to this question is
unnecessary.

___________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-08-097
Page No. 2


FACTS

You are the Trustee of the Pacifica School District located in San Mateo County.
On the June 3, 2008 ballot, a school parcel tax measure, known as Measure N, was
defeated, since it did not receive the required two-thirds of the voters' support. Because
it was a close contest, your committee would like to retain left over funds to place
another school parcel tax measure on the ballot for voter consideration.

ANALYSIS

There is nothing in the Act that would prohibit a primarily formed ballot measure
committee from retaining funds it holds on the day of the election. However, if the
committee makes expenditures to support or oppose state or local candidates or any other
California committee, including other ballot measure committees, it may qualify as a
different type of committee. This would require the committee to amend its Statement of
Organization (Form 410) within 10 days of qualifying as a different type of committee.
(Section 84103.) If the committee is used to support the qualification and passage of a
future ballot measure only, the new measure's alpha designation, when it is available
from the local Registrar of Voters, must be included in the amendment of the Form 410.
(Section 84102(d).)

For a review of the reporting requirements of a ballot measure committee whose
measure failed, please review Campaign Disclosure Manual 3. We have included a copy
of Campaign Disclosure Manual 3; Information for Committees Primarily Formed to
Support or Oppose a Ballot Measure with this letter for your convenience. We also note
that there are provisions in the Elections Code governing the use of funds raised in
connection with ballot measure elections. Although the Commission does not interpret or
enforce these provisions, they are provided in Appendix 1 of the campaign manual.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

KSM:jgl

Enclosure
